headlines.title: 2 Oakland police officers, suspect injured after traffic stop ends in shooting
headlines.description: Two Oakland police officers and a suspect ended up in the hospital Friday following a traffic stop in which the suspect attempted to flee and an officer opened fire, police said. The incident played out at about 3:15 a.m. along the 1200 block of 9th Avenue, according to police. While conducting a traffic stop, officers spotted a firearm in the vehicle, police said. The suspect proceeded to flee, dragging one officer and pinning the other between the suspect’s vehicle and a parked car. One of the officers opened fire, striking the suspect. Both officers were taken to a local hospital and listed in stable condition, police said. The suspect also was hospitalized in stable condition. Both officers will be placed on paid administrative leave, in accordance with the police department’s policy. The police department, Alameda County District Attorney’s Office and the Community Police Review Agency will conduct independent investigations into the incident. “The Oakland Police Department remains committed to transparency and understands community concerns,” the police department said in a statement. “To ensure the investigation’s integrity, information is limited at this time. More details will be shared as soon as the investigation allows, and in compliance with the law.”

headlines.title: California will lead the nation in providing free diapers to newborns at dozens of hospitals
headlines.description: California families welcoming newborns will soon receive hundreds of free diapers before leaving the hospital under a first-in-the-nation program announced Friday by Gov. Gavin Newsom. During the program’s first year, it will be offered at about 65 to 75 hospitals that handle about a quarter of births in the state and largely serve low-income patients, Newsom’s office said. The initiative will expand to more hospitals statewide, though the governor’s office did not say how many. The state has partnered with nonprofit Baby2Baby to manufacture the diapers under the label “Golden State Start.” Newsom said the plan builds on California’s effort to make living in the notoriously expensive state a little easier for families. He highlighted policies in recent years to provide students with free meals at school and make preschool free for all. “Every baby born in California deserves a healthy start in life — and that means making sure parents have the basics they need from day one,” the Democrat said in a statement. The state set aside $7.4 million in last year’s budget to roll out the initiative, and this year’s budget proposal includes an additional $12.5 million to implement the program for the upcoming fiscal year ending in June 2027. It will allow each family to receive 400 diapers in sizes for newborns and babies up to 14 pounds (6 kilograms) when they are discharged from a hospital after birth. That’s a little more than a month’s worth, since newborns on average require eight to 10 diapers a day. Baby2Baby provides families in need with essential items. The nonprofit built a manufacturing system that allows them to produce diapers for 80% less than the retail price, co-CEO Kelly Sawyer Patricof said. “California is once again setting the standard of what it means to show up for mothers and babies,” added co-CEO Norah Weinstein. The announcement comes two years after Tennessee and Delaware became the first U.S. states to offer free diapers to families enrolled in their Medicaid programs, which provide healthcare to low-income families. Tennessee families can go to pharmacies to pick up 100 diapers per month for children under two. The Delaware program, which began as a pilot before the state extended it in 2024, provides individuals with up to 80 diapers and up to one pack of baby wipes per week in the first 12 weeks of life. California’s Medicaid system doesn’t cover diapers for newborns, but it does cover the items for enrollees ages 5 and older if they have a medical need for them. Diapers are a large, ongoing expense for many families. They typically spend about $100 a month on the items per child, according to the Center on Budget and Policy Priorities, a liberal think tank. The cost burden can lead some parents to leave their child’s diapers on for too long or reuse disposable diapers, which can result in rashes and urinary tract infections, the center says. California officials are touting the new effort as a way to ease that financial strain. “The first days at home with a newborn should be focused on the love, connection, and joy of an expanded family, not stress about affording diapers,” said Kim Johnson, the state’s health secretary. “This program helps ensure families can begin that journey with greater stability and peace of mind.”

headlines.title: Highway 1 (19th Avenue) southbound closure in San Francisco
headlines.description: A weekend-long road closure is underway in San Francisco as Caltrans begins the second phase of a rehabilitation project on 19th Avenue. Crews started work early Friday morning on the major roadway, which serves as a key artery through the city. This phase focuses on the southbound direction. Two of the three southbound lanes between Lincoln Way and Sloat Boulevard are closed. Earlier this month, Caltrans work centered on the northbound side. San Francisco Apr 27 San Francisco Apr 23 San Francisco Apr 24 The timing is also different for this closure. Crews began work at 3 a.m., earlier than the 7 a.m. start during the first phase, which could impact commutes sooner in the day. Drivers are also being advised of additional closures. From 3 a.m. to 7 a.m. through the weekend, Lincoln Way at 19th Avenue will be closed, with detours along Irving Street. This is the second of three planned weekend closures for the project. Another closure is scheduled for Memorial Day weekend. Parking along 19th Avenue will also be restricted in work zones.

headlines.title: Canvas outage impacts students across the Bay Area
headlines.description: Stanford, Cal and San Jose State are just three of Bay Area schools impacted by a nationwide hack of the popular learning platform Canvas. The hacking group “ShinyHunters” is claiming responsibility for the breach at Instructure, the company behind the popular learning platform. San Jose State is also warning students and faculty that cyber criminals might have access to personal information like names, email addresses, student id numbers and user messages. “I have a project due tomorrow, so that’s already inconvenient, all of my finals start next week and they’re all almost online, so I can’t even study,” said Kennedy, a San Jose State student. For students, Canvas is where they turn in assignments, check grades and get study materials, so the outage is causing a lot of stress ahead of finals. “I knew we had an exam review today for class, I was trying to look at the study guide and I couldn’t, it wouldn’t let me go into canvas, so I couldn’t study for anything,” Noah said, another San Jose State student. San Jose State Engineering Professor and tech expert Ahmed Banafa says he’s already given his students extensions on assignments. He also warns this could lead to more phishing scams targeting students and staff. “They can send an email as if it’s coming from the IT department or from the professor of from the school and ask for information or click on this link and now, ando they can access your information or computer,” Banafa said. UC Berkeley warned its students to watch out for suspicious messages. There is still no word on when the system will be fully back up and running.

headlines.title: San Francisco firefighter gets court diversion in alleged off-duty attack
headlines.description: A veteran San Francisco firefighter facing misdemeanor charges for an alleged off-duty attack on a patron in a North Beach restaurant will undergo court-ordered diversion in lieu of prosecution, officials said Thursday. Eigil Qwist remains under investigation by the City Attorney’s office for the off-duty incident last October that triggered a civil suit against him and the second in command of the fire department, Patrick Rabbitt. According to the lawsuit and the police reports in the case, Qwist struck a part-time Marin County sheriff’s deputy from behind on Oct. 12, knocking him to the ground. The victim lost his shoe and glasses when he was knocked to the ground, according to investigators who said the incident was captured on security surveillance video. The attack, the civil suit alleges, occurred after the victim, David Gallegioni, confronted firefighters over allegations his daughter made against one of them. San Francisco Mar 20 San Francisco Apr 7 San Francisco May 1 Even though the Gallegioni lawsuit names him as a participant, police said in court documents that they considered Rabbitt to be a witness to the incident at Original Joe’s restaurant. He identified Qwist as involved based on the video footage. Prosecutors, in a statement, announced the resolution of the case. “The San Francisco District Attorney’s Office is committed to fair and ethical prosecutions in all cases,” prosecutors said. “We charge cases based on the facts, evidence, and the law. In this case, Mr. Quist was charged with a statutorily diversion-eligible misdemeanor case, and we had no grounds to object. We object to diversion in cases where we believe diversion is not suitable, for example, in cases where a defendant may have other cases pending or has been previously convicted. In all cases, we consider the individual’s conduct, regardless of who they are.” In March, San Francisco’s fire chief Dean Crispen issued an extraordinary bulletin to firefighters, urging them to “fully” cooperate with two ongoing probes into an “alleged incident.” “The City Attorney’s Office and other independent law enforcement agencies are conducting investigations into the alleged incident,” the chief wrote in a March 17 bulletin. “The Department is fully cooperating with those investigations, and I encourage all of you to do so as well.” Gallegioni’s legal filings assert that he was summoned to the bar that afternoon by his daughter and found himself confronting off-duty firefighters over something she said had happened to her at the bar. Gallegioni said in both the suit and the notice of claim that members of the group wore T-shirts with SFFD logos. The legal claim says that after Gallegioni demanded answers about the allegations, one firefighter talking to his family pointed to others in his group and warned: “Don’t mess with us.” The claim alleges “the statement and gesture conveyed,” that city firefighters “were invoking their affiliation” with the department “as a show of authority and intimidation.” The civil suit names Rabbitt as being part of the attack. But the police statement of probable cause accompanying the original criminal charges designates Rabbitt as a witness. Rabbitt acknowledged to investigators being in the bar that day to watch the 49ers game, but told them he didn’t see anyone being attacked. As for Qwist, he told investigators that was “a mutual thing,” according to the probable cause statement.

headlines.title: Berkeley renews Flock camera contract, rejects expansion plan
headlines.description: Berkeley city leaders Thursday night voted to renew the city’s contract with Flock Safety license plate reader cameras, but rejected a plan to expand the system. The City Council voted late to approve a 12-month contract renewal for the cameras already in use across the city, at a cost of about $200,000. The measure passed narrowly in a 5-4 vote. Councilmembers, however, rejected a proposal to expand the system in a separate 8-1 vote. The decision follows months of debate over whether Berkeley should continue using the technology ahead of the contract’s expiration in July. Berkeley police said the cameras help reduce crime, especially as the department faces staffing shortages. But critics raised concerns about privacy and how the data could be used, including whether it could be shared with federal agencies such as U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE). “There’s no reason for Berkeley in this climate with surveillance and ICE and everything that’s going on,” said Berkeley resident Cheryl Davila. A spokesperson for Flock Safety told councilmembers the company has changed its system to prevent data from being shared outside of California. “We shifted our entire business model. It’s not possible for any agency to share information outside of the state,” the spokesperson said. Many speakers at the meeting opposed renewing the contract, citing concerns about surveillance and Berkeley’s status as a sanctuary city. Earlier this week, nearby El Cerrito decided not to renew its own contract with Flock. Berkeley 11 hours ago Oakland Apr 28 Alameda County Apr 22 Alameda County board approves Flock Safety camera extension

headlines.title: Some passengers on the cruise with hantavirus outbreak are back in California
headlines.description: The California Department of Public Health said it has been notified by the CDC of California residents who were on the cruise ship near South America that had passengers infected with the hantavirus. “We are coordinating with local health officials, as needed, to monitor returning travelers. There is no information that the California residents are ill or infected,” a department spokesperson said, adding that it can’t share the travelers’ residences. “At this time, the risk to public health in California is low,” the spokesperson said. This comes as news spreads that at least seven people who were on that cruise have now returned to the United States. The hantavirus has killed three people and sickened five others on the cruise ship. Health May 7 Health May 6 Health May 6 The ship has continued sailing with its remaining passengers. Stephen Kornfeld, a doctor from Oregon, is a passenger on the cruise ship. He said that when the ship’s doctor fell ill, he began helping out by treating patients on board. “I just sort of fell into the role of ship physician as people deteriorated and more people got sick,” he said. Dr. Abraar Karan, an infectious disease faculty and attending physician at Stanford, explained that Hantavirus is most commonly spread from rodents to humans. “Typically, you need to be near either urine or feces of a rodent that is infected, and then you would need to be breathing that in in a very closed space,” he said. He noted that the Andes strain of the Hantavirus that was detected on the cruise ship is the only strain that is known to spread between humans. The Andes strain is typically found in South America. Karan said that, unlike COVID-19, he does not believe there will be widespread public transmission of Hantavirus. “The risk is extremely low of anyone in California coming into contact with– you know — the return travelers who’ve come back here, particularly because it’s been a very coordinated effort with the California Department of Public Health,” Karan said, noting that public health officials will be tracking the impacted passengers from this cruise.

headlines.title: World Cup brings heightened security risks. What's the Bay Area preparing for?
headlines.description: From rogue drones to cyberattacks, the Bay Area is preparing for heightened security risks as the FIFA World Cup matches come to town. There will be six matches in Santa Clara. The matches, fan events, and visits from international dignitaries will part of the largest sporting even in the world, making them impressive and potentially big targets. Mike Sena, the executive director of the Northern California Regional Intelligence Center (NCRIC) says agencies like his prepare for months, even years, for events like World Cup matches. The Bay Area, he says, had the best possible security run-through with the recent Super Bowl. However, in some ways, the World Cup is more difficult to secure because events are at various locations and over the course of multiple weeks. “Whether it be bombings, rammings, edge weapons, and now drones,” Sena said. “Also cyberattacks [from] around the globe. They don’t have to be anywhere near their target.” In February, Sena testified in front of Congress about the impacts of the U.S. Department of Homeland Security shutdown, which recently ended, and the delays in the $625 million in FEMA funds earmarked for FIFA protection. More than $51 million of that money is for the San Francisco Bay Area region. Although the money is coming through now, some agencies like his have to do what normally takes six months in two months, he said. “The first thing is always the financial piece…It’s the threat because if we don’t have the right people in place, it won’t get done,” Sena told lawmakers. And if security preparations aren’t in place, he says, agencies can’t get ahead of real threats. “We don’t have the capabilities to fight every drone threat,” he said. “I had a person who was planning on flying a drone right into the Super Bowl.” According to Sena, law enforcement received a tip and seized a drone days before the Super Bowl. The pilot’s intention was similar, he says, to the 2017 case where a Sacramento man modified a drone to drop anti-media fliers over tens of thousands of people at Levi’s Stadium and the Oakland Coliseum. Also, last November, another man flew his unauthorize drone during a football game at Levi’s Stadium. He’s now facing up to $100,000 in fines and potential prison time. The Santa Clara Police Department is the lead agency for security coordination during the World Cup matches. Chief Cory Morgan told NBC Bay Area his department fronts all FIFA security costs and relies on being reimbursed from the federal funds. “I’m confident in the people, in the personnel that are going to execute the plan. And those [public safety efforts] are going to happen independent of those reimbursements,” Chief Morgan said. For the first time, Santa Clara PD says it sent a sergeant to Alabama for the FBI’s latest counter drone training. Federal legislation that went into effect in January finally allows state and local agencies to use certain drone fighting technology and techniques that previously only the feds could. In this political climate, and with an international presence both on and off the field, Sena and Morgan said agencies will need all the security tools they can access. “We’re definitely aware of global conditions,” Chief Morgan said. “Our partnerships are key.”

headlines.title: How to watch San Francisco Giants vs. Pittsburgh Pirates on Friday
headlines.description: Giants baseball returns to NBC Bay Area on Friday, as San Francisco opens a three-game home series against the Pittsburgh Pirates. The Giants are looking to start the weekend series with a needed win at Oracle Park. Game preview: Giants vs. Pirates San Francisco has a 14-23 record overall and an 8-11 record at home. Giants hitters are batting a collective .238, which ranks 10th in the NL. Pittsburgh has a 10-8 record on the road and a 21-17 record overall. The Pirates have the eighth-ranked team slugging percentage in the NL at .387. Robbie Ray (2-4, 2.95 ERA, 1.06 WHIP, 40 strikeouts) is expected to start for the Giants, while Carmen Mlodzinski (2-2, 4.76 ERA, 1.50 WHIP, 40 strikeouts) is projected to take the mound for Pittsburgh. Friday’s matchup is the first time both teams meet each other this season. How to watch Giants vs. Pirates Friday’s game airs on NBC Bay Area, channel 11, cable 3. Coverage starts at 7 p.m., with first pitch scheduled for 7:15 p.m. For more Giants coverage, visit nbcbayarea.com/giants. Associated Press contributed to this report

headlines.title: This North Bay family has given away more than 300 cars to people in need
headlines.description: The Crozat family of Santa Rosa has spent decades turning business success into acts of generosity. Family patriarch Gene Crozat opened G&C Auto Body in 1972. As the business grew, Crozat, who died in 2016, made it his mission to share that success with others in need. “He started going to the local car mart right across the street, buying cars on Saturdays,” said his son, Patrick Crozat. “He worked with local radio stations and insurance agents to find people in need, and it just grew from there.” The effort has not only continued over the past decade but expanded significantly. To date, the Crozat family has given away 319 vehicles to people who otherwise could not afford one. On a recent Thursday, the Crozats partnered with the Auto Body Council and Allstate Insurance to donate six cars to people selected from a pool of applicants. One car went to Serena Duvall, a mother from Petaluma. Duvall said the car will allow her to reliably take her daughter to school and activities, something she had struggled to do before. “This is absolutely unbelievable,” Duvall said. “This is the missing piece. This is everything. I can’t believe I was picked. I’m speechless. This is going to change my daughter’s life and mine.” “My dad would always say this world is a ‘you and me’ world,” Patrick Crozat said. It is a lesson that his family not only learned, but one they continue to practice.

headlines.title: Charges filed against man in connection with Bay Bridge takeover
headlines.description: The Alameda County DA’s Office announced Thursday charges were filed against a man in connection with Sunday’s Bay Bridge takeover. The DA’s Office said 30-year-old Jose Mendoza was charged with two counts of felony evasion and one misdemeanor count of battery on a police officer. This comes after authorities said he allegedly drove recklessly within the blocked-off section of the bridge at high speed while a CHP vehicle pursued him. Officials said that after Mendoza slowed down, he allegedly resisted arrest by refusing commands, attempting to run away and attempting to strike an officer during the encounter. Bay Bridge May 4 Bay Bridge May 3 The Oakland Police Department said nearly 80 illegal ATVs and dirt bikes were seized in connection to Sunday’s incident and four other people are facing felony charges. They added 19 others were cited and released on misdemeanor charges.

headlines.title: Flock cameras debate continues in Berkeley
headlines.description: Berkeley is debating whether to continue using Flock license plate reader cameras ahead of the contract’s expiration in July. Berkeley is considering a two-year renewal of its Flock license plate reader contract. The decision comes less than 24 hours after nearby El Cerrito decided not to renew its own contract with Flock. Berkeley’s current contract expires in July, and the proposed renewal would keep those cameras operating. Thursday’s meeting was the continuation of a March meeting, when a scheduled vote to extend the city’s Flock contract never happened after hours of public comment. Those same concerns were front at center this time. “There’s no reason for Berkeley, in this climate, with 47 and surveillance and ICE and all these things that are going on,” said Cheryl Davila of Berkeley. Berkeley police say the technology is helping reduce crime as the department deals with staffing issues. But councilmembers pressed Flock Safety, asking what safeguards are in place when it comes to sharing information with federal agencies. During the meeting, a Flock spokesperson said “we shifted our entire business model. It’s not possible for any agency to share information outside of the state.” However, for many critics, that was not enough. “If we’re considering ourselves to be a sanctuary jurisdiction, then that means we shouldn’t contract with Flock or any ALPR for that matter,” said Manny of Alameda County.
